description: Cross-paper edges in the Maynard-Tao bounded-gaps proof DAG. Each edge connects node IDs across different papers.

edges.note: Maynard's $S_1$-side diagonalized sieve sum is the $k$-dimensional analog of GPY's 1D $\Lambda_R(n;\mathcal{H}_1,\ell_1)\,\Lambda_R(n;\mathcal{H}_2,\ell_2)$ asymptotic.
Maynard's $S_2$-side diagonalized sum (with multiplicative $g(p)=p-2$) generalizes GPY's prop:2 weighted prime count.
From $E_1 = \liminf_n (p_{n+1}-p_n)/\log p_n = 0$ (qualitative, GPY) to $\liminf_n (p_{n+1}-p_n) \le 600$ (quantitative, Maynard).

From $E_r \le (\sqrt{r} - 1)^2$ (GPY, unconditional eq:1.13; conditional bound under level-of-distribution $\theta$ is $E_r \le (\sqrt{r} - \sqrt{2\theta})^2$) to $\liminf_n (p_{n+m}-p_n) \ll m^3 e^{4m}$ for the $m$-th gap (Maynard, also unconditional).
Polymath8b restates Maynard's $H_1 \le 600$ verbatim as the baseline being improved.
Cuts unconditional gap from 600 (Maynard) to 246 by combining $\varepsilon$-enlarged simplex sieving with Polymath8a's MPZ window.

Under EH, sharpens Maynard's $H_2 \le 600$ to $H_2 \le 270$ and adds $H_3 \le 52116$, $H_4 \le 474266$, $H_5 \le 4137854$ (Maynard's $H_1 \le 12$ unchanged); under GEH, drops to $H_1 \le 6$ and $H_2 \le 252$.
Standard-simplex sieving theorem cleanly restates Maynard's $S_1, S_2$ asymptotic via $I_k(F), J_k^{(m)}(F)$.
Same $M_k > 2m/\theta \Rightarrow \mathrm{DHL}[k,m+1]$ reduction.

$\varepsilon$-enlarged simplex extends Maynard's framework — $F$ supported on $(1+\varepsilon)\cdot\mathcal{R}_k$ with marginal integration on $(1-\varepsilon)\cdot\mathcal{R}_{k-1}$. The new ingredient that beats Maynard.
Truncated-simplex variant adapted to smooth-modulus MPZ window. Couples Polymath8a with Maynard's framework.
Beyond-$\varepsilon$ under GEH: support up to $(k/(k-1))\cdot\mathcal{R}_k$ with vanishing marginals — needed for $H_1 \le 6$.
$\mathrm{DHL}[k,j]$ is the quantitative skeleton common to Maynard's prime $k$-tuples motivation and Polymath8b's reductions.
Polymath8b's thm:1.1 = GPY's thm:1 with explicit constant $H_1 \le 16$ under EH.
Polymath8b's prime-side asymptotic on the $k$-dimensional simplex extends GPY's 1D weighted prime count.
Non-prime asymptotic extends GPY's $S_1$ to $k$ variables.
Polymath8b's base divisor asymptotic is the multidimensional analog of GPY's perturbed $T^*_R$ lemma.
Polymath8b cites Polymath8a's MPZ inequality $600\varpi + 180\delta < 7$ as thm:2.5.
Polymath8b's MPZ statement is Polymath8a's $\mathrm{MPZ}^{(4)}$ restricted to the form needed for Maynard-Tao sieve consumers.
Truncated-simplex sieving requires the unconditional $\mathrm{MPZ}^{(4)}$ input from Polymath8a.
Numerical $M_k^{[\alpha]}$ bounds operate within Polymath8a's $600\varpi+180\delta<7$ region.

Polymath8a's Section 8 sieve consequence is built on the GPY (Goldston-Pintz-Yıldırım) one-dimensional Selberg-sieve framework, not Maynard's multidimensional sieve. Polymath8a was contemporaneous with Maynard and used the Zhang/GPY tradition for its sieve corollaries.
Same $I_k^{(1)}(F)$ integrals as Maynard's small-gaps sieve, here repurposed to the large-gaps covering construction.
Same $J_k^{(1)}(F)$ marginals — Maynard runs his own machine backwards.
$\log k$ optimization mirrors Maynard's small-gaps prop:4.3 part 3 — same variational inequality, opposite application.
Maynard's multidimensional sieve, originally invented to prove small gaps exist, is here pivoted to prove arbitrarily large gaps exist. Same technique, opposite direction.
Same lower bound on prime gaps ($R \log X \log_2 X \log_4 X / (\log_3 X)^2$), proved independently and simultaneously. The pair settles Erdős's \$10000 problem.

Both papers reduce to the same kind of 'cover residues by primes' problem. FGKT solves it via random covering (probabilistic); Maynard solves it via his own multidim Selberg sieve (deterministic). The structural divergence in approach.
FGKT plugs in Green-Tao linear-forms-in-primes (Theorem A) where Maynard plugs in his own $k$-D Selberg sieve (lem:6). Same role in the proof, totally different analytic engine.
thm:3.1 is the abstract version of 1311.4600 prop:4.2: any $(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{P}, \mathcal{L}, B)$ satisfying density / equidistribution hypotheses gives $\gg \log k$ primes among $\{L_i(n)\}$.
prop:6.1 is 1311.4600 prop:4.1 with $W$ replaced by general modulus $B$ and singular series $\mathfrak{S}_B(\mathcal{L})$. Architectural refactor enabling multiple applications.
$S_1$ evaluation generalized from $k$-tuples $h_i$ to arbitrary linear forms $L_i$ and arbitrary set $\mathcal{A}$.
$S_2$ evaluation under abstract level-of-distribution hypothesis.
Singular-series control replaces the $W$-trick of 1311.4600.
Sieve-weight perturbation in the abstract framework.
thm:3.5 (Chebotarev / Galois-extension primes with prescribed Frobenius) is genuinely new — opens a direction the original 1311.4600 framework didn't cover.
GPY II quantifies the qualitative $E_1 = 0$ from GPY I to $\liminf_n d_n / [\sqrt{\log p_n}\,(\log_2 p_n)^2] < \infty$.
GPY II's $S_1$ evaluation extends GPY I's prop:1 to the $\mathcal{A}$-restricted setting ($\Lambda_R$-correlations on the intersection $\mathcal{A}(\mathcal{H}_1) \cap \mathcal{A}(\mathcal{H}_2)$).
GPY II's $S_2$ evaluation extends GPY I's prop:2 to the $\mathcal{A}$-restricted prime indicator.
Perturbed $T^*_R$ asymptotic refined to the $\mathcal{A}$-restricted setting.
Maynard sharpens GPY II's $\sqrt{\log p_n}\,(\log_2 p_n)^2$ rate to a fixed bounded gap $H_1 \le 600$.
For $m$-th gaps, Maynard improves on GPY II via the $m^3 e^{4m}$ unconditional bound — different functional form, stronger statement.
Pintz survey states GPY I's conditional theorem verbatim.
Pintz states GPY I's $\Delta_1 = 0$ result.
Pintz states GPY II's quantitative liminf verbatim.
Pintz pushes $[0,c] \subset J$ using GPY II's quantitative $\sqrt{\log p_n}$ bound.
Generalized scaling $f(n) \to \infty$ uses the GPY II quantitative bound as input.
BFM applies Maynard's $M_k > 2m/\theta \Rightarrow \mathrm{DHL}[k,m+1]$ reduction to prove that the limit set $L$ hits 9-tuple differences.
Conditional improvements ($k = 9$ baseline) ride on the same enhanced sieve as Maynard's conditional gap bounds.

Modified Bombieri-Vinogradov is the level-of-distribution INPUT consumed by Maynard's prop:4.1 in BFM's application, not a specialization of it. Prop:4.1 needs an EH-type hypothesis; thm:4.2 supplies one (restricted to bounded-$P^+(q)$ moduli).
Both papers use the same refined Page-Landau zero-free region; the FMT version (lem:2.1) is the polished form, BFM's lem:4.1 the prototype.
BFM's $m$-tuple limit-point statement quantifies and extends Pintz's $[0,c] \subset J$ result.
Castillo et al: Maynard's $H_1 \le 600$ over $\mathbb{Q}$ lifted to bounded gaps in $\mathcal{O}_K$ of any number field $K$.
Function-field $\mathbb{F}_q[t]$ analog with $q$-uniform constants — stronger uniformity than the $\mathbb{Q}$ case.
Sieve weight asymptotic written with prime ideals $\mathfrak{d}$ in place of integer divisors $d$. Direct word-for-word translation.
$y$-diagonalization for ideal weights.
$y^{(m)}$-diagonalization with $g(\mathfrak{p}) = |\mathfrak{p}| - 2$.
$y^{(m)}$-to-$y$ bridge for ideals.
Selberg-type weighted ideal sum identity.
Number-field DHL reduction.
Function-field DHL reduction.

Totally-real $H_1 \le 600$ inherits the explicit $k = 105$ / $m = 2$ admissible-tuple construction from Maynard 1311.4600 (the source of the 600 constant). Castillo et al (March 2014) predates Polymath8b's 246 (July 2014).
FMT generalizes Maynard's single large gap to chains of $k$ consecutive large gaps. Key novel step: chain construction via combined deterministic and random sieves.
FMT generalizes FGKT's single large gap to chains. The Ford-Maynard-Tao paper unifies what 1408.5110 and 1408.4505 had done in parallel.
FMT's deterministic interval sieve is the chain-version of Maynard's single-gap covering.
FMT's random prime sieve is the chain-version of FGKT's random covering.
FMT abstracts FGKT's specific random covering into a general bipartite covering theorem with sparsity + second-moment hypotheses — usable beyond prime gaps.
Singular-series factorization for chained intervals extends Maynard's single-interval $I_k$ computation.
Concentration estimate extends Maynard's marginal $J_k$ computation to $k$ chained intervals.
Independence-style estimate extends FGKT's variance bound across distinct intervals.
Chain construction reuses Maynard's multidim Selberg sieve as one of two engines (the other being random covering).
Maynard's PIAP I cites Polymath8a's smooth-modulus equidistribution verbatim as its baseline.
Polymath8a needed dense divisibility / smoothness of moduli; PIAP I gets a comparable level for general (non-smooth) double moduli $q_1 q_2$ with $Q_1 \le x^{1/21}$, $Q_2 \le x^{10/21-\varepsilon}$.
PIAP I's almost-all level $1/2 + 1/55$ (any modulus) sharpens what Polymath8a's MPZ window $600\varpi+180\delta<7$ achieves only for smooth moduli.
Type II / Type III estimates extended to a strict-polytope regime in $(Q_1, Q_2)$.

Polymath8b's MPZ inequality $600\varpi + 180\delta < 7$ ran over smooth moduli; PIAP I supplies an analogous level for general moduli, in principle usable as a sieve input without the smoothness restriction.
Triply well-factorable strengthens the well-factorable hypothesis (def:1 vs def:2).
Polymath8a achieves level $1/2 + 7/300$ for smooth moduli; PIAP II achieves $3/5$ for triply well-factorable sequences. Same goal (BV beyond $1/2$), structurally different hypotheses.
Linear-sieve weights at level $7/12$ give a sieve consequence comparable to Polymath8a's prop:8.4 but via the well-factorable route rather than smooth moduli.
Sister paper in the same trilogy. PIAP I handles fixed residue classes, PIAP II handles well-factorable sums. Different attack on the same beyond-BV question.
Same author's sieve methods, applied to a totally different problem (digit-restricted primes vs. small gaps). Demonstrates the framework's generality.
Type I equidistribution at level $X^{50/77}$ uses the same dispersion-method machinery as Polymath8a's Type I estimate, repurposed for the digit-restricted set $\mathcal{A}$.
Sieve asymptotic decomposition over dyadic prime ranges parallels the multidim Selberg sieve's smooth-$F$ evaluation, adapted to a Fourier-analytic ambient set.
